text.tspan: App reads cache; on MISS, app loads from DB and populates cache.
App writes to DB and invalidates cache.
+ simple, no cache library needed beyond a key-value store
+ cache stays small (only what's been read)
+ cache failure doesn't break the app (just slower)
u2212 cold cache penalty on first access
u2212 app code is responsible for cache logic everywhere
u2212 stale-read race window between DB write and cache invalidate
USED BY: nearly everyone using Redis/Memcached
App reads CACHE LAYER (not DB). On MISS, cache layer fetches
from DB transparently and caches it. App never sees the DB on reads.
+ app code is simpler (one read API)
+ uniform caching logic in one place
u2212 requires a cache layer that supports loaders (EHCache, Caffeine, NCache)
u2212 cache layer is on the critical path; harder to bypass
USED BY: Java in-process caches; CDN read paths
App writes to CACHE LAYER. Cache writes SYNCHRONOUSLY to DB
before returning. Cache and DB always consistent.
+ no stale-cache issue (cache and DB written together)
+ reads always hit cache after write
u2212 every write pays DB latency (no faster than direct DB write)
u2212 cache must be available for writes to succeed
USED BY: caching layers in Hibernate L2, some CDNs
App writes to CACHE. Cache acknowledges immediately.
Cache flushes to DB ASYNCHRONOUSLY (batched, queued).
+ very fast writes (cache speed, not DB speed)
+ coalesces multiple writes to same key
+ smooths burst load on DB
u2212 RISK OF DATA LOSS if cache crashes before flush
u2212 read-your-writes from DB is delayed
u2212 complex failure recovery
USED BY: high-write OLAP staging; metrics counters;
write-back disk caches (CPU L1, SSD)
